question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` override mechanism to inject a `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` header on transaction responses, causing HTTP desync (request smuggling) that allows the attacker to poison shared caches with crafted transaction gossip data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.send(res.locals[responseBodyLabel])` call (line 51) after headers are already set to trigger a double-send condition if `next()` invokes another response-sending middleware, causing transaction data to be sent twice and potentially exposing it to unintended recipients in a gossip-like manner?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseBodyLabel]` being stored after serialization (line 50) to have the `responseCacheUpdateHandler` downstream cache a full transaction list response, which is then served to all subsequent users without re-validation, enabling passive gossip of stale transaction data at scale?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `NotFoundError` thrown when `responseData === undefined` (line 30) to enumerate valid vs. invalid transaction IDs through differential error responses, building a map of existing transactions for targeted gossip without triggering the normal response path?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[requestPathLabel] ?? req.route.path` fallback (line 33) to supply a `requestPathLabel` that does not match any `headers.path` key, causing `headers.path[path]` to return `undefined` and the spread to apply only `headers.default`, potentially omitting path-specific security headers that prevent transaction gossip caching?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: linkNextHeaderValue()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `linkNextHeaderValue` function emitting a fully qualified URL in the `Link` header (including host and path) to fingerprint the internal mirror node URL structure, enabling targeted gossip attacks against specific transaction pagination endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals.responseData.links?.next` value being sourced from database query results without URL validation to have the `Link` header point to an external attacker-controlled URL, redirecting gossip-harvesting clients to a malicious transaction data aggregator?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `JSONStringify` utility (line 50) handling of circular references or special BigInt values in `responseData` to trigger an unhandled exception that bypasses `responseHandler` error handling, causing the Express process to crash and creating a denial-of-service that prevents legitimate transaction gossip prevention?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals.statusCode` being read without bounds-checking (line 41) to have an upstream controller set a non-standard status code (e.g., 0 or NaN) that causes `res.status(code)` to behave unexpectedly, bypassing the `code >= 400` logging gate and hiding transaction gossip enumeration from audit logs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.set(mergedHeaders)` call (line 39) to have a controller inject a `Content-Disposition: attachment` header via `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]`, causing browsers to automatically download transaction data files that can be trivially re-shared as gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a `Vary` header in `mergedHeaders` for transaction endpoints to cause shared reverse proxies to serve cached transaction responses to users with different query parameters, leaking one user's transaction query results to another in a gossip-equivalent cross-user data exposure?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` override to inject a `X-Accel-Redirect` or `X-Sendfile` header that causes the upstream proxy/web server to serve an internal file containing transaction data, bypassing the `responseHandler` serialization and gossip-prevention logic entirely?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `req.originalUrl` being logged unsanitized (line 57) to inject Unicode or control characters that corrupt structured log parsers (e.g., JSON log aggregators), causing transaction gossip enumeration events to be silently dropped from security monitoring?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `startTime` being sourced from `res.locals[requestStartTime]` (line 53) which defaults to 0 elapsed if undefined, causing the logged elapsed time to be 0ms for all requests where `requestStartTime` was not set, masking the true cost of bulk transaction gossip enumeration requests in performance monitoring?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseDataLabel]` containing a `transactions` array with no enforced maximum size in `responseHandler` to request the maximum `limit` parameter on transaction list endpoints, receiving and re-broadcasting the largest possible transaction batch in a single gossip-equivalent response?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `headers.path[path]` lookup using a path string that contains prototype pollution payloads (e.g., `__proto__` or `constructor`) as the `requestPathLabel` value, causing the `mergedHeaders` object spread to inherit attacker-controlled properties and inject arbitrary headers into transaction responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` being spread last in `mergedHeaders` (line 37) to override any security header set by `headers.default` or `headers.path[path]`, including `Cache-Control: no-store` directives that prevent transaction gossip caching, by having a controller set permissive cache headers in `responseHeadersLabel`?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.send()` being called before `next()` (lines 51, 60) to have a downstream middleware in the chain re-read `res.locals[responseBodyLabel]` and perform additional processing on the serialized transaction data, potentially logging or forwarding it to an unintended destination in a gossip-equivalent manner?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `APPLICATION_JSON` constant comparison (line 50) being case-sensitive to bypass `JSONStringify` serialization by having a controller set `content-type` to `Application/JSON` (different case), causing raw `responseData` objects to be sent via `res.send()` with potential prototype chain leakage of transaction metadata?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals.responseData.links?.next` being read directly from `res.locals.responseData` (line 42) rather than from the `responseData` variable (line 27), creating a TOCTOU window where a concurrent request modifies `res.locals.responseData` between lines 27 and 42, causing a mismatched `Link` header that points to a different transaction page than the one returned?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.set(LINK_NEXT_HEADER, linkNextHeaderValue(linksNext))` call (line 46) to have a controller set `links.next` to a value containing multiple comma-separated URLs, causing the `Link` header to advertise multiple gossip-equivalent next-page URLs that bypass single-page rate limiting?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `responseHandler` middleware applying no authentication or authorization check before serializing and sending `res.locals[responseDataLabel]` to enumerate all transactions accessible via any route, since the middleware blindly trusts that upstream route handlers have already enforced access control?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` injection path to set a `Link` header with `rel=preload` pointing to a bulk transaction endpoint, causing HTTP/2 server push or browser prefetch to automatically gossip transaction data to the client without an explicit request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `JSONStringify` serialization of `responseData` (line 50) to trigger prototype pollution via a crafted `responseData` object where `__proto__` properties are serialized and then parsed by downstream consumers, enabling injection of attacker-controlled fields into transaction gossip data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals.statusCode` defaulting to 200 (set in `requestLogger`) without validation in `responseHandler` to have a controller that fails to set an error status code still return a 200 response with partial transaction data, causing gossip consumers to treat error states as valid complete transaction records?
